question_title: Will Turkey record a temperature of 47°C or higher between June 18 and June 28, 2026?
summary: Turkey reaching 47°C during June 18-28, 2026 is very unlikely. The threshold of 47°C has only been crossed once in June over the past 53 years (47.8°C in 2024, described as the "hottest June in 53 years"). Current 16-day forecasts show maximum temperatures of only 39-42°C in southeastern Turkey's hottest locations (Cizre, Şanlıurfa), a full 5-6°C below threshold. June 2026 has been running 2.5°C below seasonal average. The main heat surge appears to peak in early July (Cizre forecast 44.3°C on July 2), after the resolution window closes. The primary residual risk comes from: (1) model-to-station bias — MGM station readings can exceed model forecasts by 2-5°C at specific hot stations, partially narrowing the gap; (2) extended forecast uncertainty for June 25-28, where a forecast bust could push temperatures higher than currently modeled. Monte Carlo analysis across multiple sensitivity scenarios yields 2-5% probability, with a weighted central estimate of ~4%.

factors.description: Historical base rate: 47°C+ in June occurred once in 53 years (2024, 'hottest June in 53 years'). For the Jun 18-28 window specifically, ~1-1.5% unconditional annual probability.

Current 16-day forecasts show max temperatures of 39-42°C in southeastern Turkey (Cizre hottest at ~42°C), a full 5-6°C below the 47°C threshold. Short-range forecasts are highly reliable and show nothing approaching threshold.
June 2026 running cooler than average (-2.5°C below seasonal norm per easeweather), suggesting this is NOT an unusually hot June.

MGM actual station readings can exceed model forecasts by 2-5°C (June 2024: MGM 47.8°C vs model 43.3°C = +4.5°C gap; June 2025: +0.8°C gap). Only 2 data points but this systematic gap narrows the effective margin.

Extended forecast uncertainty for days 8-11 (June 25-28): forecasts could be off by 3-4°C. These later days have highest forecast temperatures (~41°C). A sudden heat ridge development is unlikely but possible.
'African heatwave' pattern flagged by meteorologists for summer 2026 but not manifesting in current forecasts. Heat surge appears to peak in early July, AFTER the resolution window.
Long-term warming trend: SE Turkey June avg max has risen +3.0-3.4°C over 30 years, making historically unprecedented readings more likely in any given year.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 4% probability (YES)**

**Key reasoning:**

Turkey reaching 47°C between June 18-28, 2026 is very unlikely based on converging evidence:

1. **Extreme historical rarity**: 47°C in June has been reached only once in 53 years (47.8°C in Şanlıurfa, 2024 — "hottest June in 53 years")

2. **Current forecasts far below threshold**: The hottest forecast for any southeastern Turkey station is ~42°C (Cizre), a full 5°C gap to the 47°C threshold. MGM's own June 18 forecasts peak at 39°C for Şanlıurfa/Batman.

3. **Below-average June**: June 2026 is running 2.5°C cooler than seasonal average — this is not shaping up as an extreme heat year

4. **Heat peak comes after the window**: The forecast shows heat building toward early July (Cizre at 44.3°C on July 2), suggesting the major surge arrives after the June 28 resolution deadline

5. **Residual upside risk (~4%)**: Model-to-station bias can be 2-5°C at specific hot MGM stations, and extended forecast uncertainty for days 8-11 allows for an unforeseen heat ridge. Monte Carlo simulation across multiple assumptions yields 2-5% across scenarios.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Turkey record a temperature of 47°C or higher between June 18 and June 28, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44104)
[Temperatures to rise across Türkiye as June heat sets in](https://www.dailysabah.com/turkiye/temperatures-to-rise-across-turkiye-as-june-heat-sets-in/news)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if any official weather station of the Turkish State Meteorological Service (Meteoroloji Genel Müdürlüğü, MGM) records a maximum air temperature of 47.0°C or higher at any location in Turkey on any day from June 18, 2026 through June 28, 2026 (Turkey local time), inclusive. The reading must be an MGM-measured value. Resolution will be based on MGM's realized highest-temperature data, published via MGM's current-situation portal at https://www.mgm.gov.tr/sondurum/extreme-temperatures.aspx, or on MGM-attributed figures reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). A single qualifying reading anywhere in Turkey during the window is sufficient (a value of 47.3°C would resolve YES).
fine_print: "47.0°C or higher" means the reported maximum air temperature at a standard MGM weather station meets or exceeds 47.0°C. Readings from non-MGM sources (e.g., private weather stations, satellite-derived land surface temperature, or unofficial sensors) do not count toward resolution. If an MGM figure and a credible news report conflict, the MGM figure prevails. If no qualifying reading of 47.0°C or higher is reported by July 5, 2026, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Historical base rate: 47°C+ in June occurred once in 53 years (2024), making this roughly a 2% annual event for all of June. For the specific Jun 18-28 window, perhaps ~1-1.5% unconditional
Current 16-day forecasts show max temperatures of 39-42°C in southeastern Turkey (Cizre hottest at ~42°C), a full 5-6°C below the 47°C threshold. Short-range forecasts (3-5 days) are highly reliable.
June 2026 running cooler than average (-2.5°C below seasonal norm per easeweather), suggesting this is NOT an unusually hot June
MGM actual station readings can exceed model forecasts by 2-5°C (June 2024: MGM 47.8°C vs model 43.3°C = +4.5°C gap; June 2025: +0.8°C gap). This systematic gap narrows the effective margin.

Extended forecast uncertainty for days 8-11 (June 25-28): forecasts could be off by 3-4°C, and these later days have the highest forecast temperatures (~41°C). A 5-6°C exceedance is still unlikely but not impossible.
'African heatwave' pattern flagged by meteorologists for summer 2026, but not showing in current forecasts for this specific window. Heat surge appears to peak in early July (after resolution).
Long-term warming trend: SE Turkey June avg max has risen +3.0-3.4°C over 30 years, pushing extremes toward thresholds more often

input.anchor: Historical base rate: ~2% per year for 47°C+ anywhere in Turkey during all of June (once in 53 years per question description). For the specific Jun 18-28 window (~37% of June, but the hotter portion), roughly 1.5% unconditional.
input.assessment:
This question asks whether an extremely rare temperature threshold (47°C) will be crossed during an 11-day window in late June 2026. The key evidence overwhelmingly points toward NO:

1. **Historical rarity**: 47°C in June has occurred only once in 53 years (2024, the "hottest June in 53 years"). Even accounting for climate change, this remains a tail event.

2. **Current forecasts far below threshold**: The hottest forecasted temperature for any southeastern Turkey location during Jun 18-28 is ~42°C at Cizre. This is 5°C below the threshold. Even accounting for model-to-station bias (which can be 1-5°C based on limited data), the gap is likely 1-4°C.

3. **Below-average June**: June 2026 has been running 2.5°C below the seasonal average, further reducing the probability of extreme heat.

4. **Heat peak timing**: The forecast shows heat intensifying toward early July (Cizre forecast 44.3°C on July 2), suggesting the major heat surge arrives AFTER the resolution window.

5. **Quantitative model**: Monte Carlo simulation combining prior distribution of June peaks, Bayesian updating with current forecast information, and station bias yields 2-5% across sensitivity scenarios, with weighted average ~2.6%.

The main residual risk comes from extended forecast uncertainty (days 8-11) and the possibility that the model-to-station gap is larger than expected. But bridging a 5-6°C gap between forecast and threshold requires an extremely improbable combination of forecast error and station bias.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor of ~1.5% is the unconditional base rate. Current forecasts provide strong information that updates slightly upward (to ~4%) due to model uncertainty in the extended range and station bias, but the below-average June so far partially offsets. No hedging toward 50% - the evidence clearly supports a low probability. The 4% estimate is at the lower end of the 2-5% range from the Monte Carlo analysis.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Model-to-station gap: Only 2 data points (June 2024: +4.5°C, June 2025: +0.8°C) for this critical parameter. If the gap is consistently 3-4°C, the effective margin narrows significantly. 2. Extended forecast reliability: Days 8-10 forecasts can change substantially. An unforeseen heat ridge could develop. 3. Whether 'African heatwave' pattern materializes during the window rather than after it.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Very productive - returned comprehensive data from Open-Meteo forecasts, historical archives, MGM API data, and wttr.in. Three parallel research queries all succeeded. Web search: Returned mostly generic travel weather pages, but one useful finding (easeweather noting June 2026 is cooler than average). Fetch URL: Confirmed Daily Sabah article has no extreme heat signals. Coherence links: Empty (no related questions). Code execution: Successfully ran Monte Carlo and sensitivity analysis.
input.update_triggers: 1. Short-range forecasts (5-day) showing 44-45°C+ for Cizre/Şanlıurfa would move probability to 15-25%. 2. Any actual reading above 44°C during the window would indicate conditions approaching threshold. 3. GFS/ECMWF ensemble showing sudden heat ridge development over southeastern Turkey.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was excellent for this question, providing both forecast data and historical context in a single call. The Monte Carlo simulation framework was helpful for combining multiple uncertain parameters. Key friction: the model-to-station gap is critical but I only have 2 data points, making the prior distribution highly uncertain. A tool that could access MGM historical station data directly would have been valuable. The decomposition approach (modeling the underlying quantity first, then deriving the crossing probability) worked well for this threshold question.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Systematic Review

### Resolution Criteria
✅ Resolution criteria were retrieved via `get_metaculus_questions` and are fully specified. The agent engaged with the specific terms: MGM station readings, 47.0°C threshold, June 18-28 window, and the MGM portal as authoritative source.

### Factor-by-Factor Verification

**Factor 1 (Historical base rate, logit=-3.5):** The claim "47°C+ in June occurred once in 53 years (2024)" is sourced from the Metaculus question background, which states: "In a recent 'hottest June in 53 years,' Şanlıurfa reached 47.8°C on June 20." The agent noticed its own research contradicted this (finding "All known 47°C+ temperature readings in Turkey have occurred in July or August — never in June") and transparently reconciled this by noting the Open-Meteo archive may not capture MGM station-specific readings. Grounded.

**Factor 2 (Forecasts 39-42°C, logit=-1.5):** Supported by Open-Meteo 16-day forecast data showing Cizre max ~41.9°C on Jun 19, and by wttr.in 3-day forecasts, and by MGM API forecast peaks of 39°C for Şanlıurfa/Batman. Grounded.

**Factor 3 (June 2026 cooler than average, logit=-0.5):** Supported by easeweather.com snippet from web search: "Until now, June 2026 in Turkey is cooler than usual, with temperatures averaging 24.4°C, this is -2.5°C below the typical seasonal average." Grounded.

**Factor 4 (Station-model gap, logit=+0.8):** Uses June 2024 gap (MGM 47.8°C from question vs Open-Meteo 43.3°C = +4.5°C) and June 2025 gap (MGM 45.6°C from question vs Open-Meteo 44.8°C = +0.8°C). Both values traceable to specific sources. Confidence appropriately low at 0.5 given only 2 data points. Grounded.

**Factor 5 (Extended forecast uncertainty, logit=+0.5):** Standard meteorological reasoning about forecast degradation at 8-11 day lead times. Supported by the observation that the highest forecast temperatures (41°C at Cizre) appear in the later portion of the window. Grounded.

**Factor 6 (African heatwave pattern, logit=+0.3):** Sourced from Metaculus question background: "Meteorologists have flagged elevated heatwave potential for summer 2026 amid an 'African heatwave' pattern." The agent searched for independent confirmation and found none. Confidence appropriately very low at 0.4. Grounded in question text.

**Factor 7 (Warming trend, logit=+0.3):** Supported by worldview research key_fact: "Southeastern Turkey June average maximum temperatures have risen +3.0 to +3.4°C over the past three decades." Grounded.

### Double-Counting
No significant double-counting detected. Factors 4 (systematic station bias) and 5 (forecast lead-time uncertainty) address related but distinct phenomena — one is a systematic offset, the other is random error that grows with lead time.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All factor signs are consistent with their descriptions. Negative logits correctly assigned to evidence pushing toward NO; positive logits correctly assigned to evidence pushing toward YES.

### Contradictory Assessment
The narrative assessment clearly and consistently points to NO, aligned with the heavily negative factor balance. The quantitative Monte Carlo analysis (2.1-4.6% across scenarios) is consistent with the stated 4% probability.

### Tool Audit
The agent's audit mentions "Three parallel research queries all succeeded" — the tool metrics show 1 research call (which contained 3 embedded queries), so this is accurate. The stated "9 calls, 0 errors" matches the ground truth metrics exactly. No disagreement.

### Counterargument Quality
The counterargument is substantive: it identifies the poorly-sampled model-to-station gap (only 2 data points), forecast bust potential for days 7-10, and climate change frequency effects. It specifies five concrete scenarios that would shift the probability. Not a token gesture.

### Self-Assessment Confidence
Confidence of 0.55 is appropriate given thorough forecast data and Monte Carlo analysis, tempered by acknowledged thin evidence for the station bias parameter and inherent extended forecast uncertainty.

### Probability Assessment
The agent's 4% probability is consistent with the quantitative analysis (weighted average ~2.6% across Monte Carlo scenarios, with an upward nudge for model uncertainty and tail-event conservatism). The evidence strongly supports a low probability: forecasts 5°C below threshold, June running cool, heat peak arriving after the window. An independent estimate would fall in the 3-5% range, consistent with the agent's 4%. No meaningful divergence.

### Anchor Divergence
Anchor of ~1.5% to final of 4% is a modest ~2.5pp departure, justified by the station bias factor (+0.8 logit), extended forecast uncertainty (+0.5), and other upward adjustments. No concern.

### Worldview Consistency
The three Turkey temperature research entries in the worldview store are the same ones generated by this session. No contradictory forecasts or research entries found.
